title: Simone de Beauvoir interview in English (1976)
text:
take a slate two take one
[Music]
throughout her life simon de beauvoir
has defied convention
at 17 when other middle class parisian
girls were strictly controlled at home
she went to the predominantly male
sorbonne to study philosophy
in 1929 at the age of 21 she flouted the
prevailing morality by living
unwed and openly with jean-paul sartre
the noted existentialist philosopher
who has perhaps been the greatest single
influence on her life
with him she took a leading part in
leftist political causes in france
in 1969 when the notion was highly
controversial
she advocated women's right to abortion
on demand
she has been an idol of the feminist
movement ever since her book
the second sex was published 25 years
ago
at the time the book was considered to
be violently radical
and it sold twenty two thousand copies
the first week
de beauvoir has had a long career as a
controversial playwright
novelist and essayist today in her late
sixties
she's world renowned
recently because she wanted to share her
views with american women
she granted permission for wned to film
an interview in her paris apartment
provided the tv crew was all female
the interviewer is an american friend
dorothy tenoff
professor of psychology at the
university of bridgeport
[Music]
what specific events led to your
becoming a feminist
being feminist i was in a way when i
wrote the second sex
because i began to be very interested in
the
feminine condition at this time that is
20 years ago
but then there was no kind of
organization
of a feminine action which really
interested me
and i began to be
much more active only this last year
when i met the liberation movement the
roman liberation movement
what we call in france the mlf movement
liberation
farm then i was interested by their way
of
fighting and i was interested in them
too
and i began to take part to their
fight what particular issues
interested you oh all the problems of
the feminine condition
are involved in each other and the wall
scene interested me
but i began with the question of
abortion because
in france we are very very late very
reactionary on this question and the
abortion is a terrible problem for the
woman
and one woman when they wrote to me
freedom for the woman begins in the womb
and i thought it was very true and when
some
young woman came and asked me to sign a
manifest saying i had been aborted in
past times
and we were three hundred for this re to
tell it
well i accepted and we began a long
walk about abortion first the long walk
for abortion
occurred on the 22nd of november 1971
and marked a turning point for both de
beauvoir and the women's movement in
france
for her it signaled a re-entry into
active participation in the movement
[Music]
it looks like being a huge success it's
mobilized many women over a problem of
the utmost importance to them that of
abortion
when i think about the present situation
regarding abortion in france
which forces the vast majority of women
to risk their futures by having
secret abortions with bits of parsley or
celery stalk at the peril of their lives
in the most ghastly conditions it
deserves the strongest protest
and i'm delighted with our protest of
today
[Music]
because she brought her prestige to the
movement the long walk attracted
attention around the world
and helped lead to the recent change in
french law that permits abortion in the
first ten weeks
three members of the french feminist
movement who are especially close to
simone devoir
michelle claudine ser
and becky
we had no idea that it would really
become something really important from
the french women
and known all over the world and none
all over the world and the greatest
thing also
is that from from my experience i can
say is that french for the french woman
the word abortion was a really terrible
word
and starting this manifesto everybody
started talking about it and as long as
simone de beauvoir had signed it you
know the women really were concerned
and they really filmed some kind of new
solidarity that they have never felt
before
did this produce a change in the
abortion laws the change was elsewhere
it was in the private illegal
movement which began to
appear in paris but
in other towns to in grenoble and
a woman taking on herself
the the work of making abortions a woman
and
even some doctors helped to and it was
important
for the the fact that a woman did that
illegally
saying they did it and the police did
not
dare to intervene do you
uh work within any organizations
no i don't work in organization i i work
with some woman of the liberation
well on one scene or one other it was
once
an abortion another thing was
a great feast we made at the mutuality
another thing could be a march in uh
streets in paris
or i can help in some little ways for
abortions
for instance by letting my my place
to the doctors and the patients who come
for that
well written things like that but and
then i am
in very good terms with some women of
the movement
but themselves they are a little
different they are different i shall not
say sex but it must be the same thing in
the states
they are different groups which don't
always agree
with each other so i am very friendly i
am in very friendly terms with
some of these groups but not with all
there are some of them i don't
know at all and i guess there are some
of them
which don't like me so much neither
well i think they respect her even if
they don't agree with her they they have
to respect her
you know because she's very valuable how
about women who are not feminists do
they respect her too
maybe some of them you know who are not
feminists are
kind of still a little afraid
of what she did because uh
it's still very hard for women to live
the way simone de beauvoir lived
elizabeth gould davis has written a book
that she calls the first
sex and she's a great admirer of yours
but she wonders
whether you really think that women or
you really thought that women were the
second sex
well that's a way of speaking the second
sex
it was a little like a
plane with the words because very often
i don't know if you say that in the
states too
you speak of the third sex meaning the
homosexual
so i thought it was amusing to call
the woman the sudden sex because
in fact they are a citizen of second
class
they are kept down by the men they are
certainly not on the top of the of the
society
so it was a plain with the words and
saying something
which is true now today
defines us that way not that we're that
way before i came here i spoke to
women feminists in the united states
barbara love for example
said that she felt when you wrote the
second sex
that you saw lesbianism as a kind of
pathology
and she wonders whether she's written on
this subject
your views about female homosexuality
have have changed recently
i have never thought that lesbians were
pathological on the contrary i told
very clearly that homosexuality was a
choice for a woman
as good as any other one and that she
could put her freedom
in this relationship with another woman
and never thought it was pathological
uh such feminists as kate millett
and naomi weinstein and others have
talked about psychotherapy as
a tool of oppression against women i
guess it's true
freedom to say that freud was used
to persuade women that they had to be
mothers and to stay home and
to play their feminine role and i
am quite of the idea that the psycholis
in particularly has been a way of
oppressing women used by men in many
in many cases i don't say in all cases
but in many cases
it has been used to oppress for men and
to make women
stay
dutch for instance who explained that a
woman were masochist
which is very easy for the man to
believe because so they have
they can be oppressor and sadist without
remorse
since it is said that you women are
masochists which i don't
think at all so i agree with you get me
that and you and all
about the bad news of psychotherapy
regarding women
since she stopped work three years ago
on her latest book of memoirs
de beauvoir has become more active in
her support of the women's liberation
movement
she is president of schwazir a
pro-abortion group in paris
she's president of the league of rights
of women which deals with discrimination
against women
she writes about issues concerning women
for latomodan
a left-wing paper in paris often using
her writing to encourage
other women to write about themselves do
you feel that the
impassioned intense jealous
infatuation that you've described in
your novels and your memoirs an
inevitable
part of being a woman no i think there
are women who are not
jealous who are not infiltrated who are
not who have not all these
that would see default because i have
what it is
do you think women can be independent
emotionally
who can be independent emotionally as
men to have their emotions and
it's not a bad thing to our emotions and
their feelings and
to care for other people for instance or
situation in the world indeed that i
don't know exactly what we mean by
independent emotionally
women have such strong traditions of
mutual distrust
and suspicion and not getting along with
each other very well um and we talk
about
sisterhood in the united states which i
understand
uh we in france of translation
has not been found possible
it says there is nowhere to say
sisterhood which is a very
beautiful world but well there is
sisterhood
among a lot of women i know they are
very friendly and they help each other
and
they are really they work in sisterhood
but
not all of them not all of them is a
sister with all of them
what do you think women fear most about
feminism
i don't know if it is fear they are
jealous of the woman
who are not just the kind of servant and
the slaves and the objects
themselves i guess that is that they
fear her to feel a
inferiority in regard with this woman
who walk outside and they will do as
they went and
who are free and maybe they are afraid
of the freedom which
is made possible for them because
freedom is something
very precious but in a way
a little fearful because you don't know
exactly what to do with it
so i guess that's what some women fear
you think it's basic then it's not just
a matter of
misunderstanding what feminism is
no i guess even if it is not
misunderstanding
there is a more basic fear than that
certainly
most of them don't want to be really
free
and they are afraid that men would not
be the same with them and would not
protect them and help them
what they don't do in fact but the woman
hope that you will do
what do you think men fear most about
feminism
they fear a lot they fear to have
confidence in their work outside the
home
they fear not to have a servant at home
to make all the things which are useful
for them
and they feel to lose their feeling
of superiority because it's always very
pleasant to feel superior to
somebody so i guess many of them feel
fear all these things together
most of them i guess fear not to have a
servant at home that's the most
important thing for them yes
they will accept a woman outside to walk
outside
if she does exactly the same kind of
work
for them in the furnace in at home
how about very recently since you've
become a very active feminist
and since there's a movement of a
feminist movement
has have men's reactions intensified
become stronger about this
i guess there is more violence today
among the men that then there was in my
time
because they recent the
indeed the will of women to liberate
themselves
and the foremost men sexual freedom that
means
so you can sleep with me you ought to
sleep with me
and i know that many of my friends are
much more
annoyed and they don't know how to say
in the streets
than i was or my sister or my friend
when we were young
the men are much more arrogant and they
follow women and they insult them
if the woman will not go with them more
than in past times
that was always something existing in
paris indeed
and in italy chiefly but now it's worse
than it was certainly
and it is certainly for a kind of recent
month
a friend of mine has wondered about
prostitution
she thinks that it could be a
respectable profession among women
if men didn't control it and if the pay
were right
what do you think about that i is it
very much i don't
know maybe it could in any way it would
be
the way you say it could it would be
paid enough and it would not be
controlled by men
then if women wanted to do that
after all they are free to do it i don't
know
i don't feel that it is a very good way
of living for a woman in a way
she's too even in this good condition
she's
too dependent on the desire of men
no i am not very much fault
what do you think the greatest obstacles
are to feminism
well it is what we said uh if you used
before
it is the fear men have and the fear
women have
and it's the wall scene and that makes
feminism
difficult do you think that that
technological
changes have made feminism possible
now where it may not have been possible
before
uh washing machines birth control i am
not
sure i am not sure that it is really
the basic scene for feminism
because you can have a birth control in
the washing machine
and be an oppressed woman and you can
have no washing machine
and no birth control and manage to be
your own master uh wilma scott heidi who
is the president of the national
association for women now wondered
whether you feel that it is very
important
to bring out feminine qualities
in in men that our culture has
not permitted to exist and also what we
might call masculine qualities in women
i don't believe at all that there are
natural
qualities which are masculine or
feminine culturally
yes for instance women are less
in general in a general way less
aggressive
less violent less important to and
full of the idea of their
dignity and went in on earth and all
that
usually that's the men who are chiefly
like that competitive and all that
so if it would be possible to make the
men less aggressive less competitive
less sure of themselves
that would be certainly something good
and if
you could help a woman to be
more sure of themselves more
trying to rely on themselves and not on
other people not relative
to men but having themselves
in in their own hands that would be very
good too and these are things that men
might
want themselves to there there could be
feminist men couldn't there
do you think there will ever be many
feminist men many no i don't think there
are many
but uh i should say i know some of them
were really feminist
chiefly in the very very young
generation
man of 2025
[Music]
more really feminists because they feel
the
question of age against
the old people the mature people again
adults
more than the discrimination from sex
you have always been a woman

do you feel that you have been within

no i never felt i was discriminated well
i was very astonished when i wrote
the second sex to see that some of my
male
friends were very angry at me for
instance camus
i believed he was if not a feminist he
considered that
women and the men could be equal
and then i discovered him and the some

that they had a feeling of superiority
and was very angry
at a woman saying the things i said in
the second sex
but before i never
saw in my circle in a mark of
discrimination since
i wasn't literally as good as
any other do you think that your
writings and your work would be
different
if you were not a woman and i mean that
in the sense
of when women
step into the domains of
of thinking that that men assume are
their own
they have more trouble there are very
few women philosophers for example
i cannot think of myself as not being a
woman
because i cannot think i am not myself
so myself is being a woman and indeed my

uh extreme express this
situation with mine situation of being a

uh to be a philosopher that i spoke
about that in my
memoir and uh two in second sex
uh it seems to me that it is a very
seldom that
every man is a royal great important

so it seems to me natural not to be a

a little in natural trans exceptional is

but that was never the sin i wanted i

i always wish it to to be a writer and
she free to write novels
but not to be uh like kant or
something like that you have repudiated

through your life have you found a
and felt a need for a substitute
no i mean human activity on this earth
seems to me quite sufficient to to live
de beauvoir has said that the idea of

that she someday must die later as she

she decided to face the problem head on
she wrote coming of age
a large work about old age and because

in your book the coming of age you

some people have said is was a taboo
subject
old age yes and you talked about the

but also about the indifference and the

yes would you like to give us some

recommendations
that you feel would help but my idea is
that
you can do nothing for elderly people if
you have not
begun to do something for the people
from
the youth into the old age the drama of
most all the people already
is that they have been very
[Music]
worn out you say they have worked too
much in
too bad conditions and when they are
60 65 and they come to retreat they are
very very old much older than people
who have not worked all their life and
to not only they are very
holding their in their body but
they have not the possibility of
afraid enough interest in themselves
they have no culture
usually most of them and so they don't
know what to do
with their last years of
their life when they have a little time
to do something but
they don't feel like doing anything most

in the town but in the town and chiefly
for the working class
it's a terrible drama chiefly for the
men
because the women can always do little

while a man if he's taken away from

and he feels himself
no longer a man it's a very very

how do you feel that medical professions
uh
function to increase or decrease

it helps a lot of people to come to

as it was in past times i mean in our

relatively rich society
and then there are much more older
people
than there were before but
i don't think that medicine make really
much
for the low-class people
they help chiefly
reach people to uh to
to keep a body in the same conditions
but not the not the poor because it's
very
very costly to take care of one's
health and all people cannot really do
that
we've had a lot of difficulty in our

too
on the issue of death and this is one
that you have raised in your writings
in the coming of age and some
magnificently in
a very easy death the story of your

what feelings do you have about that
do you separate it from your feelings
about
old age yes this is something
different since it can happen to young

and then it is particularly horrible
when you have
young people taken away without having
had
their lives but
there too there is a social difference
so i say that it never is a death i see
my mother
had not a hazy death in fact but she was
with her children she was in good
conditions
she did not feel herself die she did not
know she died
in fact but poor people when they are in

because they are not enough nurses
nurses are not bad but they are
too they have too much work to do and
the doctors too and
so they are very lonely and they know
they are going to die and they have a
terrible
dish and i feel longish is
something more terrible than this itself
if i could have a wish i should wish
to die without english without i didn't
know without knowing maybe knowing but
not with this terrible english that very
many people have when they feel they are
going to die and they feel
so lonely yes many people feel that that
they would prefer to know
but the horrible thing that happens
which i think you just depicted so well
in the book is the isolation
that comes because of everyone's fears
yes
yes because there are lies between
people
who even between people who usually tell
everything to each other if one lies to
the other
because he knows he's going to die for
instance and the other does not know
that's a terrible thing it puts you out
of contact with everyone yes
yes have you begun to feel your own
influence
on other women well a lot of them tell
me i had influence
i hope it's true and it pleased me very
much when i feel it's
real certainly yes i think i had

in so much that they wanted somebody to

them i don't think the book changes the

of another person except if
if she is waiting in a way for this book

just difficult to say everybody tells me

was important for a woman so i like it
but personally i care maybe
more for my books of sunni
really memoir because after all the

in a way could have been written by

and in fact a lot of women now write on

and very near the way i wrote myself
but my memoir that i alone could write

however uh read any of the recent

a very good english book by julia

and well i read many books and
some articles rather written by french

well there was a very important thing i

that was the importance of the economic

working as inside the house what they
call the hidden walk
and i did not know how millions of
hours of this work there is in france
and in the states too and how it is

to have women doing this work for
nothing not being paid for that
and i discovered that all the laws about

and all that was in connection with
this exploitation of women at home and i
guess that is a very important scene
and now a lot of women begin
to take conscience off that were there
any women that were important
uh figures in your life in your earlier

zaza i spoke uh in my first
book of sunni but
then she died very young and i had good

friends or i had younger girls
for whom i felt very
much affection and i had my sister but i

either of them had the intellectual

in fact there is just one person it
happens to be a man but
i just lived in a kind of suburus
intellectually we saw because we spoke

from uh morning to night and
and we made our ideas together and a lot
of fights
although there have been other men in

existentialist philosopher jean-paul

with whom she has completely shared her

today he's ill and nearly blind but she

she watches over his health and privacy

we have not been influenced by each

uh about uh or should i say the

the creation the creative part of our

uh sark read it and they gave me

and gave him advice but the first

was
always done by each of us alone
it was only after it was done that we

and then it might have been changed
because of what you would
say to each other yes very often i
heard his advice and the change what i

my advice and determined what he has
written it was quite asymptotical
would you be willing to tell us how you

i don't feel nothing better it seems

it can happen and some people are really

but uh if somebody uh went to have a

she must have them who thought marriage

oh yes but i i always thought what could

people disappear
in my eyes that would be the best scene
people manage a way of getting children
and
having these children happy but without

what we call the nuclear family which is

first and said very well that is what i
like very much in her book
sheila master yes she talked to about

what does that mean to you do you think

feminism yes certainly because
liberating the children it's liberating
to
the woman the mothers and then it is

the little girls for their future life
not being
their mother has been i guess that's
very important it's very important to
give the children
responsibility very very young
in the world society and not to be just
involved
in the family i heard i am not sure it's
quite true but i heard that
in china uh very very little
children are useful
to for doing little work in uh
manufacture and all that and
they earn a little money and they have
the conscience of me not just
the child of the parents but a little

collectivity and i guess that's very

in other words they're able to
contribute to the society
at an early age whereas yes they don't
feel they are just
the thing of their parents they are not
an object the woman
has objects but the children too and if

changed that would be a very very good
thing and certainly
for all the women too how do you feel
about

feel
now it is such a duty to have children

are told that it is so
from the youngest years
a girl is said she has to be a mother if

but when they grow up some of them
maybe many of them begin to feel that

the earth does not need to have more and

so it can help the mentality of the
woman
do you think that if we had a changed

that men uh would enjoy being parents
more it's possible that you it's very
difficult
to speak of the future i always fear to
say
nonsense when i pick up but
what it seems to me is that again the
young generation
they many of you men take care of the
children much more than before
and now you can see that and you see a

with children and shoulders and all that
certainly
that's part of their feminism that's to
their relationship to the children
in some couples indeed not in many but
among the hippie for instance
that can happen what are some of the
more significant events
that are occurring in technology in

in social conditions are there some that

i don't know for the future i know for
the present there are a lot of things
which are
frightening what happens in chile what
happens in greece what happens
all over the world that seems to me
something very frightening
what seems important to me
maybe the most important thing is the
maybe the progress the effort of people
who were not free before
to become free but it's a long way and
it's very difficult
i mean africa for instance asia all
these countries
which begin just now if in africa
to be their own masters but
it is very difficult in the condition
where they live
to make the necessary
progress to have all the people living
happy
that seems to be important and very
difficult
and what is frightening well it is the
the role of the
strengths which go against this
liberation
of the people all this uh fascists

what is your life like now this is not
did not change it since my last book

as i translated that in english by all
